data.title: Logical Skepticism For Peace Pulls The Petrodollar Higher - EUR/USD, AUD/USD And Dollar Index Overview
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The US dollar (DXY) has been in the spotlight over the past two months, after remaining the pet peeve for FX since early 2025.

With wartime, however, things can change fast and decisively: the global reserve has risen by 4.40% since its end-January lows in a blink.

The new Fed Chair elect, hawkish repricings, Petrodollars, and a general backing away from risky trades built up sudden demand, particularly at a time when asset managers were the most bearish on the USD in 14 years. And when the market is stuck on one side of a trade, it often results in huge reversals.

The Petrodollar trade, however, was the fuel for the currency market throughout this month, and despite what seemed like a relative dissonance this week, as the tone sours again, the correlation is coming right back.

Indeed, after cautious optimism throughout this week's trading following the announcement of the US-Iran talks, it seems that Iran is still heavily inclined to maintain its strategic advantage over the Strait of Hormuz and its ballistic missile capabilities - two of the most contentious points in the indirect talks.

But the real issue for markets is that President Trump's tone has gradually grown more pessimistic, and this coincides with the fact that the huge Marine fleet is arriving in the Arabian Sea in less than a day. So where are these mere distraction tactics?

Good question, as war strategy is something that us common mortals cannot fully grasp. The Art of War, legendary treatise, can help in that sort (and is also a great read for traders).

Deception tactics are common, and nobody can really understand what any side has in mind.

What is sure, however, is that WTI is now rebounding toward $95, the higher bound of its momentum pivot, and above the psychological level, sentiment will sour even further.

At least, the US dollar is enjoying this move, amid a more hesitant FX session. As further clarity is expected in the next 24 hours, consider current levels as indications of doubtful neutrality amid the ongoing war.

Anything above in oil and the dollar implies a worsening in conditions and sentiment, which should drag into next week (+ Pessimistic)

On the other hand, a return below $90 in WTI will be most welcome to investors (+ Optimistic)

We will look at the Dollar Index, EUR/USD, and AUD/USD to assess the current state of the market and whether more upside is warranted for the dollar amid resurfacing doubts.

Dollar Index 4H Chart

The US dollar attempted a corrective sequence as the US-Iran talks were announced, but with WTI not correcting much further, the currency still receives fundamental support.

A few technical elements are developing in the Dollar Index which can help to find trades in major FX pairs:

  • A short-term, relatively flat bear channel has developed and contained the index movement. Traders may either use it as points of entry to long and short the US dollar or as a breakout signal. We are currently reaching its highs (99.90).
    • Its lows are at around 98.65.
  • A more neutral 99.00-100.00 range is holding for now. It is less responsive but more stable to watch the higher time frame movement.

Levels of interest for the Dollar Index:

Resistance Levels

  • Morning spike 99.93 and channel top
  • Weekly range highs 100.00
  • 100.00-100.50 main resistance zone
  • War highs 100.544

Support Levels

  • Intraday micro support 99.30
  • 98.70-99.00 support (mini range lows)
  • 98.00 2025 support
  • Support 97.40-97.60
  • 2025 lows 96.40-96.80 support

AUD/USD 4H Chart and Technical Levels

AUD/USD just broke its past month's 0.6970-0.7150 major range to the downside, indicating high potential for a further correction.

Fundamentals are changing for the Aussie dollar after a strong period after two consecutive hikes from the RBA, particularly after Australian CPI surprised to the downside.

Failing to rebound above 0.6980 would hint at more bearish activity in the major pair.

Levels of interest for AUD/USD:

Resistance Levels

  • Dec 2021 lows 0.6970-0.70 major pivot (broken range holds above)
  • Mini resistance 4H MA 50 – 0.7020; bullish above
  • 2023 highs from 0.71-0.7150 resistance
  • 0.71867 March highs
  • June 2022 extremes 0.72-0.7230

Support Levels

  • 0.69-0.6935 early Feb support
  • 0.69015 session lows
  • Micro support 0.6850 (+/- 30 pips)
  • October 2024 mini-support 0.6750 (+/- 100 pips)

EUR/USD 4H Chart and Technical Levels

EUR/USD is now turning bearish, breaking its 4H 50-period MA after a double top this week and crossing below its pivot zone (1.1540-1.1570).

This hints at a test around at least the 1.1475-1.15 November support, which could extend further to the 1.1410 war lows if the situation deteriorates.

Levels to place on your EUR/USD charts:

Resistance Levels

  • Immediate resistance 1.1546
  • Resistance 1.16250-1.16350
  • 1.1650-1.17 March Resistance
  • 1.1750 mini-resistance and channel top
  • Resistance zone around 1.18 (+/- 150 pips)

Support Levels

  • 1.1475-1.15 November support
  • War lows 1.1410
  • 1.1350-1.14 support
  • 1.12-1.13 next main support zone

Safe trades, and keep a close eye on Middle East developments!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ECF: Remain Cautious In This Sideways Market
data.description:
Overview

While market indices continue to trend sideways, investors have rotated out of technology in search for safe havens. The Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund (ECF) provides investors with better price stability due to its blended portfolio of equities and fixed-income securities. When I previously covered ECF, I issued a hold rating due to the sensitivity to higher interest rates at the time. Since then, the Fed has initiated several interest rate cuts and the fund has released an updated annual report. Therefore, I wanted to reassess the fund's overall value proposition and outlook for 2026.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, ECF's share price has increased by 21.2%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to participate in the positive market momentum over the last few quarters. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 30.2% over the same time frame. ECF now offers investors a starting dividend yield of roughly 6.7%, which can be appealing for investors looking to produce a higher-than-average dividend income within their portfolio. However, the dividend income can be quite sporadic over time based on how well ECF's earnings are during any given time frame.

The fund still trades at a reasonable discount to NAV valuation, but I am still cautious about the accumulation of shares. The outlook remains positive with some renewed growth catalysts but the success of the fund relies on positive market momentum. If the indices continue to trade sideways, the fund may fail to capture the necessary level of earnings to support payouts. Despite this, the fund still provided a bit of protection against the recent market volatility.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, ECF now has total net assets of $201M that are spread across a diverse range of blended securities. The fund's primary goal is to provide attractive total returns and put an emphasis on high distributions. In order to achieve this goal, ECF allocates most of its assets to a blend of convertible securities and common stocks. The convertible securities are hybrid instruments that generate income similar to a bond, while the fund also sells off the upside growth of its common equities to help support the higher payouts.

A benefit of this approach is that ECF can efficiently provide protection against periods of market uncertainty, similar to what we are experiencing right now. The income-focused securities will generate income when the equity markets are trending sideways. Similarly, the inclusion of common equities means that ECF can partially participate in the upside growth of market rallies. When it comes to constructing the portfolio, management implements some different strategies to ensure exposure to the highest quality positions. After reviewing management's latest commentary, I was able to highlight some important metrics for management:

  1. Locate securities with asymmetrical return profiles.
  2. Protection against market declines.
  3. Discounted opportunities.
  4. High conviction on attractive outlook.

What I like about the fund is that it has a primary focus on energy and utilities at 19% of assets. These are the sectors that tend to have stable cash flows through different market cycles, since they offer the necessary services that improve the quality of our lives. Energy and utilities tend to deliver slow but reliable growth so this is a great area for ECF to plant the foundation of its portfolio for a steady stream of income generation.

However, the fund doesn't have a set rebalancing schedule so an position in ECF means that you are putting confidence in management's ability to keep tabs on all of its holdings. When looking at the positions, we can see that ECF provides exposure to some of the highest quality companies in the world. For instance, ECF has common equity exposure to holdings like Broadcom (AVGO), Hewlett Packard Enterprises (HPE), Microchip Technology (MCHP), and Microsoft (MSFT), just to name a few. Similarly, ECF has income-focused positions in other well-known companies, including Lumentum Holdings (LITE) and Alibaba Group (BABA).

Performance Outlook

When assessing the outlook of the fund, I want to circle back to the fund's primary allocation to energy and utilities. While these were historically thought of as slow but reliable growers, I believe that the sector has a renewed catalyst of AI growth. The rising demand for AI has caused a spike in the amount of power needed to support higher energy consumption. Furthermore, the outlook for AI data centers is extremely positive and positions ECF for growth over the next few years.

For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI data center market has the potential to grow to a value of $810.6B by 2033. From 2026's estimated value of $180.6B, this represents a potential CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 23.9%. With the rise of AI data centers, energy and utility companies are unique aligned to service the power demands.

Furthermore, the fund uses leverage in order to amplify its earnings over time. The use of leverage currently amounts to 15.14% of assets, which is equivalent to approximately $30.2M. I consider this to be a very moderate use of leverage and it shouldn't substantially increase the risk profile of the fund. However, the higher interest rate environment has likely negatively impacted the fund's operating spread between its cost of leverage and the yield collected from securities.

Assuming that interest rates will eventually trend lower over the next twelve months, this should improve the fund's operating spread and lead to higher income generation from its positions. However, there's always a risk that the Fed delays future rate cuts. For instance, the Fed decided to leave rates unchanged during their most recent meeting. In a scenario where the equity markets continue to trend sideways, the fund may struggle to capture the necessary growth needed to sustain the recent price rally.

On a more positive note, the fund's higher distribution may help it outperform more standard index-tracking ETFs if markets continue to trend sideways. For instance, ECF's total return over the last six months sits at 2.55%. The fund has successfully outperformed the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) over the last six months, which helps to demonstrate the appeal of a blended strategy.

Structural Risks

At the time of my last coverage, ECF traded at a discount to NAV of 11%. Despite the fund's pullback over the last few weeks, the fund still trades at a similar discount to NAV of 10.12%. While this may seem like a good price to NAV level to accumulate, the graph below indicates otherwise. Referring to the red line below, we can see that ECF now trades at the higher end of its historical price to NAV range since 2022. However, market indices like the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq-100 also trade at the higher end of their historical range as well.

The valuation can be a bit tricky when looking at the internal workings of the fund. The latest annual report for 2025 reveals that net investment income amounted to $0.28 per share, while net realized gains landed at $3.30 per share. Although a high distribution is a key part of the fund's appeal, the underlying net investment income makes up a fraction of the distributions being paid. Therefore, I would love to see net investment income levels substantially improved going forward so that the fund's reliance on net realized gains is reduced.

Since ECF relies on net realized gains, this makes it extremely difficult to estimate how the fund will perform on a forward looking basis. For instance, market indices raised upward throughout 2023, but ECF was only capable of locking in net realized gains of $0.20 per share. This means that ECF isn't necessarily guaranteed to capture large net realized gains through bull markets.

The variability of earnings also means that the fund suffers through market downturns. If market indices are trading lower, the fund may be forced to sell off common equities at a loss in order to support the distributions. For instance, the fund experienced large net realized losses during 2022's market decline, which ECF's NAV still hasn't fully recovered from. For instance, the NAV declined from $14.57 in 2021, down to $13.08 at the end of the 2025 period.

Lastly, the high dividend yield doesn't automatically guarantee that the fund will have high returns over time. The fund's emphasis on high distributions and limited exposure to common equities has historically led to underperformance against the indices over the last decade. For instance, ECF's total return of 210.6% over the last decade has severely underperformed against SPY and QQQ. So if you aren't an investor that needs the income, you would be better off focusing on a more standard growth ETF for a longer holding period.

Non-Traditional Dividend Growth

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.19 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.7%. However, you may notice below that the dividend history shows some sporadic payout amounts. Occasionally, the fund will issue a large supplemental distribution at the end of the year when there are excess earnings. This system is great because it rewards shareholders during strong market rallies. However, it can also be argued that the fund would be able to deliver better NAV growth over time if it retained those excess earnings instead of paying out. Either way, it's a nice bonus for investors seeking a high level of income.

Additionally, the fund has demonstrated its ability to generate earnings that exceed distributions. For instance, ECF generated earnings of $3.58 per share for the 2025 period, which was more than enough to support the annual distributions of $0.59 per share for the period. In fact, earnings were so strong that they can comfortable support several years worth of payouts:

2025's earnings of $3.58 per share / $0.59 annual payout = 6.06 years of coverage.

While it may not be a big deal for some investors, these large supplemental payouts can create sporadic dividend growth over time. To demonstrate this, I ran a back test of an initial $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. The graph below assumes that no additional capital was invested after the initial $100K, but it does assume that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of shares.

2017 Dividend Income: $5,422

2025 Dividend Income: $19,050

Lastly, investors need to consider the risk that the distributions received from ECF do not offer tax-efficiency. The same annual report reveals that distributions are supported by the combination of net investment income and net realized gains, which means that a portion of the income is likely classified as ordinary dividends and have the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in ECF is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am maintaining my hold rating on ECF at this time. The fund trades at the higher end of its historical price to NAV range and ultimately relies on the ability to capture net realized gains. If market indices continue to trade sideways over the remainder of 2026, the fund will struggle to capture the necessary level of earnings to support NAV growth and distributions. Despite this, the fund still manages to provide a bit of protection during this period of volatility and uncertainty, while also aligning itself to participate in the expansion of the AI data center market through its energy and utilities exposure.

data.title: Bayer: Turnaround Is Underway - Don't Miss It
data.description:
Bayer’s (BAYZF) stock was actually a great investment in 2025 and outperformed many other German (and U.S.) stocks and in the 2025 Annual Report, the company was able to print a chart we did not see often in recent years.

In my last article, which was published in December 2025, I rated the stock as a “Buy” and in the conclusion of my last article I wrote:

I still remain confident that Bayer will manage to turn the business around. And Bayer doesn’t need to grow with a high pace to be fairly valued right now. Additionally, if Bayer should be able to resolve its glyphosate litigations and just normalizing its free cash flow levels to previous amounts would be enough to make the stock a bargain.

However, on the one hand, we should not rule out the possibility of another huge disappointment. The Supreme Court might rule against Bayer, which would most likely tank the stock again. On the other hand, the balance sheet with high debt levels and huge amounts of goodwill remains a big problem.

All in all, I stay bullish about Bayer at this point as the picture seems to be improving and the stock is still trading for such low valuation that all the negativity is already priced in.

When my last article was published, Bayer was trading for $36.30 and now – at the time of writing – the stock is trading for $38.50 resulting in about 6% growth. In the meantime, the stock increased even to almost $50 but declined again in the following weeks.

About three weeks ago, on March 4, 2026, Bayer reported full-year results for fiscal 2025 and it is easy to see the results as a major disappointment again. Despite disappointing results, I will explain in the following article, why we should remain bullish about Bayer. Not only does the stock remain undervalued, developments in the last few weeks will most likely reduce uncertainty about the litigation process and the fundamental business of Bayer is also performing well.

Fiscal 2025 Results

We start by looking at fourth quarter results and we see sales declining from €11,729 million in Q4/24 to €11,438 million in Q4/25 – resulting in a 2.5% year-over-year decline. However, Bayer is still facing FX headwinds and at constant exchange rates and adjusted for portfolio, sales increased 3% year-over-year. Core earnings per share also declined from €1.05 in the same quarter last year to €0.62 in this quarter – resulting in a decline of 41.0% year-over-year. Free cash flow also declined 12.7% year-over-year from €3,312 million in Q4/24 to €2,891 million in Q4/25.

When looking at full-year results, the picture is quite similar. Sales declined from €46,606 million in fiscal 2024 to €45,575 million in fiscal 2025 – resulting in a 2.2% year-over-year decline. But once again, when adjusted for portfolio and FX, sales increased 1% year-over-year. Core earnings per share declined slightly from €5.05 in fiscal 2024 to €4.91 in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 2.8% year-over-year decline. And finally, free cash flow declined 32.9% year-over-year from €3,107 million in fiscal 2024 to €2,084 million in fiscal 2025.

When looking at the different business segments, we also see the three different segments struggling. Sales declined for Crop Science (2.9% year-over-year decline to €21,622 million), for Pharmaceuticals (1.7% year-over-year decline to €17,829 million) as well as Consumer Health (1.2% year-over-year decline to €5,802 million). When looking at EBIT before special items, Pharmaceuticals reported a small increase of 0.7% year-over-year to €3,391 million. However, EBIT for Crop Sciences declined 3.2% year-over-year to €4,188 million and EBIT for Consumer Health declined 0.8% year-over-year to €961 million.

And although management is pointing out that it fully delivered on its raised financial ambitions, the results reported for fiscal 2025 are certainly not great.

Light At The End Of The Litigation Tunnel

In a large part, these mediocre results are still the result of the litigations Bayer is still facing. And in the case of Bayer, it certainly makes sense to look at adjusted numbers (the core EPS) as the litigation expenses are really disturbing the picture. When looking at the “EPS walk” we clearly see the huge negative impact the litigation currently has. In fiscal 2025, litigation expenses drove down earnings per share by €7.59.

And litigations not only had a huge negative impact on earnings per share, but also on free cash flow. While we know that expenses for litigations were €7.5 billion in fiscal 2025, this number is including provisions as well and we cannot assume that free cash flow would be €7.5 billion higher without the litigations. However, it is reasonable to assume that Bayer’s free cash flow would be rather in the mid-single billions without the litigations.

In my last article I already indicated that the picture seemed to improve for Bayer, but in the last few weeks the company reported several positive developments. In February 2026 it was reported that Monsanto offered a settlement, and on March 4, 2026, the plan was preliminarily approved by the Circuit Court of the City of St. Louis. The plan includes declining capped annual payments for up to 21 years totaling up to $7.25 billion and this long-term payment stream will provide greater certainty for Monsanto and Bayer.

As a result, Bayer increased the provision and liabilities for litigation from €7.8 billion (according to a previous guidance) to €11.8 billion. And as Bayer is expecting litigation payouts of €5 billion in fiscal 2026 (first estimates), the free cash flow is expected to be negative in fiscal 2026. During the last earnings call, CEO Willian Anderson stated:

And as we shared 2 weeks ago, we're expecting a negative free cash flow this year due to litigation-related payouts. That outlook is emblematic of the company's current strategic position, strong signs of progress, but still working on a comprehensive turnaround. We've made major gains across the company, but that work is not yet complete. We're focused on delivering what we've committed for '26 and making the right long-term decisions to set Bayer up for sustained profitable growth. We have a clear picture of what needs to be done in every area. We're dialed in on the tasks at hand, and we're ready to deliver.

While this settlement will lead to underwhelming results for fiscal 2026 once again, the risks in the years to come would drop substantially for Bayer as well as its investors. Some litigation risks would remain – even if a settlement is reached – as some claims have been excluded from the settlement. But with the settlement in place and huge provisions already existing, we can assume that Bayer will generate a much higher free cash flow again – starting in fiscal 2027.

Fundamental Business

In the last few years, when writing about Bayer, we are often focused on glyphosate and the litigation and legal aspects and often forget that Bayer actually has a very compelling business model. With the litigation (probably) being settled to a huge extent, we can focus on the fundamental business again. And as we saw by looking at the results above, the business is also struggling a bit when it comes to the top line but is still growing. We can start with the company’s outlook for fiscal 2026 and rather focus on the top line as the bottom line (earnings per share and free cash flow) is heavily affected by litigations.

When looking at sales in constant exchange rates, the Crop Science business as well as Pharmaceuticals is expected to grow sales between 0% and 3%. The Consumer Health business is expected to grow between 0% and 4% in fiscal 2026. Additionally, EBITDA margin before special items is expected to be a little lower in fiscal 2026 while Crop Science is expected to improve its margin from 19.4% in fiscal 2025 to 20% to 22% in fiscal 2026.

And while management is slightly optimistic for fiscal 2026, it is even more optimistic for the next few years – especially for the pharmaceutical business segment. During the 44th Annual J.P. Morgan Healthcare Conference, Stefan Oelrich, the head of the pharmaceutical division made the following statement:

I think you can see that not only have we rejuvenated our portfolio, we have also demonstrated superior launch excellence. The amount of hits that we're having is certainly, I think, commendable and is a driver for near-term growth. And we expect to have mid-single-digit growth in the years going into the 30s, essentially starting as of '27. So '26 is sort of like the last flattish year because we still will have the full impact of Xarelto until it hits its sort of like bottom.

And as reported during the last earnings release, sales for Kerendia increased 109% year-over-year to €264 million in quarterly sales. With annual sales of €829 million in fiscal 2025 the pharmaceutical will most likely reach blockbuster status in 2026. Another pharmaceutical growing with a high pace is Nubeqa, which reported €702 million in sales in Q4/25 – 69% higher than in Q4/24. Bayer itself estimated already back in 2022 that peak sales for Nubeqa will be at least €3 billion. Peak sales of Kerendia are also estimated to be €3 billion. Compared to fiscal 2025 of these two products, we get an upside of €3 billion in sales – and we can assume these estimates are rather cautious.

In my last article, I already pointed out the launch of Lynkuet in the European Union, which is expected to reach peak sales of at least €1 billion. As far as I can tell, Bayer did not report Lynkuet sales for Q4/25 as the product is only available for sale a few months now. Of course, we should not ignore that the declining Xarelto sales pose a huge risk for the business and sales will most likely decline further – annual sales of only €1 billion are certainly possible. Additionally, Eylea will also face pricing pressures in the coming quarters with the market entry of biosimilars. In fiscal 2025, it also generated €3,110 million in sales – posing a huge downside risk at this point. It also seems possible that sales will be as low as only €1 billion in the years to come.

Nevertheless, management is optimistic that revenue for the pharmaceutical segment will increase in the years to come and management also outlined a 5-year framework for the crop science business in 2025. In 2029, Bayer is expecting sales to be at least €25 billion. While this is resulting only in low single digit growth in the next few years, margins are also expected to improve. In fiscal 2025, the margin for the segment was below 20%, for 2026 margins are expected to be between 20% and 22% and in 2029 margins are expected to be in the mid-20s. This is contributing to bottom line growth. And for 2029, management is expecting free cash flow from Crop Sciences to be around €3 billion from this segment.

Management is also expecting for the overall market to continue growing in the years to come. However, growth is expected to be only in the low single digits – only about 2% growth in the next few years.

Finally, the consumer health business can be seen as the most stable and predictable business among the three segments and we can assume growth rates also being at least in the low-single digits in the years to come.

Dividend

With outlook improving, we can also be rather optimistic for the dividend in the years to come. For fiscal 2025, the company once again proposed an annual dividend of €0.11 – similar to the previous two years. In fiscal 2023, management already announced it will pay out the legally required minimum for three years.

We can be optimistic that the dividend will be higher next year and although we should not expect a dividend of €2.80 again (it was this high in 2019), but a dividend somewhere between €1.00 and €2.00 does not seem unreasonable in my opinion. But of course, this remains speculation as management did not really talk about the dividend next year.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a final step, we calculate an intrinsic value for Bayer and for the first time, we can be a little more optimistic again. As always, we are calculating using a discount rate of 10% (the annual return we like to achieve at least) and 982 million shares outstanding. Now, we assume a negative free cash flow of €2 billion for fiscal 2026 (midpoint of guidance) and then assume the picture slowing getting better. For fiscal 2027 we assume €3 billion in free cash flow (more or less in line with previous years) and for fiscal 2028 we assume €5 billion in free cash flow (in line with pre-crisis levels).

For the years following fiscal 2028, we assume a moderate (but reasonable) growth rate of 4% annually. When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of €70.81 for Bayer – making the stock clearly undervalued at this point.

In my opinion, these are reasonable assumptions. They are neither too optimistic nor too pessimistic. Bayer might very well perform even better and free cash flow might even be higher in the years to come.

Conclusion

Bayer remains a “Buy” in my opinion, and after several years with high levels of uncertainty we can be more optimistic again and actually try to calculate with reasonable assumptions for free cash flow in the years to come. Without provisions for litigations, free cash flow seems much more predictable again.

Even when assuming that Bayer will not really grow and only keep its business stable in the years to come, the stock is still undervalued. Being a little more optimistic and assuming that Bayer will grow in the low-to-mid single digits again, the stock is deeply undervalued and certainly a “Buy”. Of course, we should not be too optimistic as litigation risks remain and the patent cliff for the pharmaceutical business is not over. Nevertheless, I think we can be more optimistic today than at any point in the previous quarters.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Banco BBVA Argentina: Operational Excellence At The Worst Point In The Cycle
data.description:
In my previous coverage of Banco BBVA Argentina S.A. (BBAR), I had highlighted my bullish case as follows:

"[BBAR]...represents one of the most balanced exposures to the new Argentine economic cycle post-Javier Milei—a system that seeks to replace the rentier and inflationary model with a real credit model, based on efficiency and fiscal discipline."

After years of hyperinflation and banks living off a completely distorted real credit landscape, President Javier Milei's ultra-liberal agenda brought down inflation and made fiscal adjustments in a short period of time. The country's banking system was forced to operate with a real credit model—i.e., production-based economic activity, income, and consumption.

As the regime transition cycle is not fast and is still in a stage of extreme credit pressure, forcing banks to increase provisions and thus pressuring margins and profit, the base case scenario today is for a recovery in ROE at least one quarter longer than anticipated, which, in my view, justifies BBAR's poor performance throughout this year.

In any case, I still remain constructive on the thesis in the long term, anticipating that not only BBAR, but the Argentine incumbent banks in general are arguably at the most challenging part of the macro cycle at this time, and normalization tends to be more visible as it proceeds in the second half of 2026.

The End of Easy Profits Is Now Hitting the System

Especially in the Argentine banking system, the macro cycle usually works as follows: (1) the economy improves; (2) inflation falls/interest rates fall; (3) profit falls first (end of carry trade); (4) credit worsens; (5) provisions rise; (6) only then does ROE start to grow again.

For years, Argentina's banks operated in a somewhat "artificial" model where hyperinflation led to very high nominal interest rates, and with that, banks carried indexed government bonds and assets that repriced quickly. The result of this regime was almost "automatic" profits flowing into their balance sheets, given the inflated margins, with gains from inflation and monetary position boosting the headline numbers.

With the Milei administration bringing inflation down sharply (from 211% per year pre-Milei (2023) to 31.5% in December 2025) and the overnight Repo rate (interest rate) converging to 29% (versus 126% in December 2023), the consequence is the end of the carry, with the structural margin of banks plummeting.

Counterintuitively, credit tends to become riskier with the fall in interest rates in the short term, because it comes along with fiscal adjustment and a drop in real income, which is why it is relatively normal to see delinquency rise even in a scenario of lower interest rates.

In the case of BBVA Argentina, 4Q25 was heavily impacted by credit deterioration, with NPL 90+ at 4.18% vs. 1.13% in the same quarter last year.

But as the chart below shows, this is a movement in the Argentine banking system as a whole; BBAR’s NPL is only higher than Banco Macro’s (BMA) among peers. So much so that loan loss allowances came in at ARS 805.1 billion (up 31% QoQ and the whopping 181.2% YoY), mainly driven by the continued deterioration of the retail book. This also made the cost of risk (adjusted for inflation) practically double from 4Q24 to 4Q25, reaching 8.11%.

The Most Painful Part of the Cycle Is Happening Now

That being said, the Argentine banking cycle is arguably entering its most painful phase.

The worsening of credit quality and the consequent sudden rise in provisions led to profit destruction in 4Q25. BBAR's net income was down -43% YoY in the quarter and annualized ROE at 7.3% vs. 12.5% in 4Q24.

The elevated cost of risk points out that, generally, defaults are still to come, and with that, the cycle is not yet close to turning. So much so that the management team confirmed in the 4Q25 earnings call that 1Q26 will still be a challenging quarter in this regard.

So, I would say that bulls would look at the current thesis of Banco BBVA Argentina, as well as the other domestic banks, and support an argument that this is all part of the cycle, and tends to be a temporary movement until the peak of delinquency is reached—this being an initial phase of an adjustment shock.

But on the other hand, bears look at the thesis through the lens that the deterioration may last longer (as BBAR itself signaled in the earnings call), and ROE may be compressed for longer than previously expected.

In my view, there is a logical order of events for the macro cycle to unfold.

The first two stages, fiscal adjustments (economy improving) and falling inflation, have already materialized, so it could be fair to say that it's quite proven. As inflation and interest rates fall, bank profits tend to fall in tandem, while credit worsens and provision increases. I believe that 4Q25 showed we are precisely in the last two stages before a turn of the cycle. ROE usually only starts to recover once credit stops deteriorating and provisioning pressure starts to ease.

So, I see the poor performance of Argentine banks' ADR recently tied to their ability to recover faster from this weak cycle, delayed at least by one quarter.

The BBVA Argentina's management team at least reinforced in 4Q25 an aggressive real credit growth guidance of 25-30% for FY26, as well as ROE returning to low-to-mid-teens, and also the cost of risk converging to 2024 levels (~5%).

I would also say that this is a scenario where a lot of patience is required. 1Q26 should still be a troubled quarter, but from 2Q26 onwards, a gradual improvement can be seen, and 2H26 is likely to point to a visible normalization. Of course, this guidance assumes a scenario in which (1) inflation continues to fall; (2) real income improves; and (3) credit stabilizes. In other words, Banco BBVA Argentina still depends a lot on the macro scenario recovery, which is clearly the main risk, but at the same time, arguably the main driver of the BBAR thesis.

The Best Bank in a Still Weak Environment

Specifically on the BBAR side, it is encouraging that today the bank has the best credit quality among its peers, as the graphs below indicate, and continues to have an above-average ROE even with the exploding provisions and the end of carry.

In addition, the capital position is very comfortable with no solvency risk and with BBAR being able to absorb losses.

BBVA Argentina also trades at just 1.23x trailing book value, levels that are even more discounted than its peers, Grupo Galicia and Banco Macro. I particularly think the valuation is still demanding, especially when compared to LATAM peers such as Banco Bradesco (BBD), Santander Brasil (BSBR), and Itaú Unibanco (ITUB), which are currently in a much more stable economic cycle and have stronger fundamentals than Argentine peers. But on the other hand, it must be agreed that there is more optionality in the thesis of Argentine banks as the country's economy moves towards a scenario of low inflation, and the resumption of the credit cycle.

Risks and Final Remarks

I remain constructive on Banco BBVA Argentina's thesis, anticipating that among its peers, BBAR is the Argentine incumbent bank with the best key metrics, which also reinforces that operational execution has been good as far as possible in the middle of a weak cycle.

However, I anticipate that the main driver of the thesis continues to be the Argentine macro, especially with inflation potentially converging to 17.6% to 24% annually, and with that, lower rates and real income supporting a scenario further ahead of credit recovery.

The weak cycle seems to be close to a peak (potentially in 1Q26), and I believe that buying BBAR while it is priced in a bad cycle may be a good idea, although caution is advised in investing in equities from a country with very high political risk and still a poor credit rating.

data.title: Disney: Undervalued IP Franchise And Robust Monetization Trends - Reiterate Buy
data.description:
I previously covered Walt Disney Company (DIS) in January 2026, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating then, given the excellent margin of safety from the cheap valuations, the management's robust forward guidance, and the healthier balance sheet.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the DIS stock, albeit after a further correction to the $90s or $86s, with the post-FQ1 2026 selloff already triggering the richer upside potential to my bull-case long-term price target of $171.

My optimism is further attributed to their ability to successfully monetize their Intellectual Property [IP] franchise across the movies/streaming/theme park/cruise offerings, with the underwhelming FQ1 2026/mixed FQ2 2026 prospects well balanced by the management's reiterated FY2026/FY2027 adj. EPS growth guidance.

DIS Faces Numerous Headwinds & Tailwinds

Since my last Buy rating, DIS has lost another double digits of its value to continue trading sideways along the resistance levels of $125s and the support levels of $80s since August 2022, with it presenting its mixed investment thesis despite my prior optimism and my historical Buy ratings.

1. Bottom Line Headwinds In FQ1 2026

Part of the headwinds may be attributed to the tougher YoY comparison on DIS' FQ1 2026 performance, with the diversified media/entertainment company delivering a weaker than expected FQ1 2026 adj. EPS performance at $1.63 (-7.3% YoY) and cash provided by operations at $735M (-77% YoY).

Part of the bottom line FQ1 2026 underperformance is attributed to:

  • the higher costs related to the Avatar: Fire and Ash theatrical release in December 2025, the higher streaming licensing fees, and the lower political advertising - with it triggering the Entertainment segment's lower operating margins of 9.4% (-6.2 points YoY), and
  • the programmatic costs related to new sports rights - with it triggering the Sports segment's lower operating margins of 3.8% (-1.2 points YoY),

all of which are triggering near-term noise in its overall operating margins at 17.7% (-2.7 points YoY) despite the higher revenues by +5.2% YoY.

At the same time, given DIS' guidance of higher sports rights expenses, the elevated pre-launch/opening costs related to new cruises/attractions, and the "international visitation headwinds at our domestic parks" in FQ2 2026, I am of the opinion that the margin headwinds may persist for a little longer before potentially recovering from FQ3 2026 onwards.

2. Reiterated FY2026/FY2027 Guidance

Part of my optimism is attributed to the DIS management confidently reiterating their FY2026 adj. EPS growth guidance at double digits and cash provided by operations at $19B (+4.9% YoY).

These developments underscore their confidence surrounding the multi-year investment trends across their movie slate/theme parks/cruises, especially since these are expected to drive their robust growth prospects, as similarly observed in the management's reiterated double-digit adj. EPS expansion through FY2027.

Part of their success may be attributed to DIS' highly successful IP franchise beyond the movies to the theme park experiences, as observed in Zootopia 2 grossing over $1.7B globally while driving higher attendance to the Zootopia theme land in Shanghai Disneyland.

The same has been observed in the higher theme park bookings by +5% for 2026, albeit heavily weighted in H2 2026, with it underscoring their likely to be robust Experience top/bottom line performance beyond the revenues of +6.2% YoY and adj. operating income of +6.1% YoY reported in FQ1 2026, thanks to the higher attendance at elevated rates.

Beyond the movies and theme park/cruise experiences, DIS' Subscription Video On Demand [SVOD] has also benefited from the robust IP franchise, as observed in the growing subscription revenues at $4.42B in FQ1 2026 (+12.7% YoY) and the robust advertising monetization cadence at $922M (+3.8% YoY).

It is unsurprising, then, that its D2C segment is increasingly profitable at operating margins of 8.4% (+3 points YoY), with further operating leverage likely as they expand in scale.

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain relatively stable, with DIS expected to deliver excellent top/bottom line growth at a CAGR of +5.2%/+11.3% through FY2028, respectively.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +7.6%/+24% and 10Y CAGR of +6.1%/+1.4%, respectively.

3. Selloff Triggers Discounted Valuations

Thanks to the -14.5% correction post-FQ1 2026 earnings call and their profitable growth trends, I am of the opinion that DIS is a lot more compelling at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 10.29x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 14.97x.

This is down from the recent highs of 12.70x/21.36x, the 5Y mean of 17.04x/29.79x, and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 11.46x/18.19x, respectively.

When comparing DIS' FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 10.29x and the LTM adj. EBITDA margin of 23.4% to its direct streaming/cruise peers, including:

  • Alphabet (GOOG) at 16.40x/FY2025 adj. EBITDA margin of 37.2%,
  • Netflix (NFLX) at 23.63x/FY2025 adj. EBITDA margin of 30.2%,
  • Roku (ROKU) at 19.66x/FY2025 adj. EBITDA margin of 8.8%,
  • Amazon (AMZN) at 10.98x/FY2025 adj. EBITDA margin of 20.3%, and
  • Paramount Skydance (PSKY) at 6.53x/FY2025 adj. EBITDA of 15.9%,

along with

  • Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL) at 8.12x/FY2025 adj. EBITDA margins of 27%,
  • Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) at 12.28x/FY2025 adj. EBITDA margins of 39.2%,
  • Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd. (NCLH) at 8.41x/FY2025 adj. EBITDA margins of 27.8%, and
  • Viking Holdings (VIK) at 15.86x/FY2025 adj. EBITDA margins of 28.7%, respectively,

I am of the opinion that DIS' discounted valuations from the ongoing correction and the rich adj. EBITDA margins from the diversified media/entertainment offerings present value-oriented investors with an excellent dip buying opportunity.

This is especially since DIS still boasts a relatively healthy balance sheet at a net debt to LTM adj. EBITDA ratio of 1.82x as of FQ1 2026, compared to the Entertainment sector median of 2.66x, with it underscoring their ability to incrementally invest in their diversified media, theme park, and cruise growth prospects moving forward.

This is on top of their likely to be secure dividend investment thesis, based on the LTM Free Cash Flow generation of $7.05B against the annualized dividend obligations of $5.2B, with the robust fundamentals underscoring why the recent correction is merely linked to the mixed FQ1 2026 optics and the deteriorating sentiments.

DIS Is An Even Better Buy After A Further Correction

For now, DIS' breach against the November 2025 floor of $100s implies that the stock is likely to continue retracing to retest the intermediate support level of $90s, as per the established trading pattern since August 2022.

Thanks to the correction, it goes without saying that DIS is now oversold at current levels, as observed in the impacted RSIs and the low trading volumes, with a bottom likely to materialize soon.

This conjecture is not overly aggressive when compared to my base-case fair value estimates of $86.80, based on the discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 14.97x (cheap compared to the 5Y pre-pandemic P/E mean of 18.19x and the peer group mean of ~21x) and the LTM adj. EPS performance of $5.80 (+5.4% sequentially).

Otherwise, based on the consensus FY2028 adj. EPS estimates of $8.18, there remains an excellent upside potential of +26.9% to my base-case long-term price target of $122.40.

This is not even taking into account the potential upgrading in DIS' valuations nearer to its 5Y pre-pandemic P/E mean of ~18x and/or peer group P/E mean of ~21x - all of which implies richer capital appreciation prospects to my bull-case price targets of $147.20 or $171.70, respectively.

Beyond the +50% dividend hike for FY2026, the management has also signaled up to a $7B share repurchase target in FY2026, with it building upon the -1.6% of its float retired over the LTM and -2.6% since FQ1 2023.

As a result of the profitable growth prospects from the diversified media/theme park/cruise offerings, the improved margin of safety/discounted valuations from the selloff post-FQ1 2026 bottom line underperformance on a YoY basis, and the robust shareholder returns through the secure payouts/share retirement, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the DIS stock.

This is, albeit, upon a bounce near my Buy Zones at the intermediate support level of $90s, barring which, a further retracement to the August 2024 floor of $86s is not overly bearish as well, given the rather pessimistic market sentiments.

Some patience may be quite prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Dynex Capital: Intact Earnings Engine Supports A Buy Despite Rising Yields
data.description:
Dynex Capital (DX) has seen some selling pressure over the past month that has made its yields more lucrative but warrants a review of the fundamentals. Part of the pressure is because of a market-wide sentiment drag because of geopolitical uncertainties. A more relevant offshoot of the Iran war uncertainties is that oil prices, and hence inflation, could remain stickier than was being expected before the war, and hence a higher-for-longer interest rate regime ahead.

However, a deeper dive into Dynex's fundamentals clearly shows that the earnings engine remains structurally intact. The recent compression reflects a rise in long-term yields, which has pressured mortgage valuations and book value. While the rate uncertainties and negative sentiments are real, I do not see sufficient deterioration in earnings that warrants caution, so Dynex is a Buy.

We are in a regime of higher asset yields, more stable funding markets, and a more supportive policy stance—that should reduce the odds of corrections that challenge the Buy thesis. The current drop in prices (and valuation reset) could therefore signal a good opportunity to capitalize on the risk-return asymmetry. It is not a screaming Buy, but can be accumulated at current levels with an eye on the rate risks.

Rate Risks, Yield Cushion and Valuations

Dynex operates a leveraged agency RMBS model, where the real drivers are interest rates, funding costs, and spreads. That means book value moves with rates, while earnings depend on how stable the borrowing and income spread remain.

Long-term yields have spiked a bit, reverting to January 2026 levels after declining to below 4% in February/early March. This is not a spike that has meant the highest seen yields in the past year or so, but the extent of stress is unknown at this point, and importantly, the declining trajectory we were witnessing before has been challenged.

As yields rise, mortgage-backed securities reprice lower, which directly impacts the mark-to-market value of portfolios held by mortgage REITs. This justifies book value pressures and potentially lower valuation multiples as well. The key question is whether that expectation is supported by the current structure of spreads, funding, and policy. The price-to-book ratio, constructed using the reported quarterly prices and book values per share, shows that at current prices, Dynex is trading at ~0.92x its reported book value as of Q4 2025. That discount to book value is not really a screaming value position, looking at how the ratio has been even in the ~0.6-0.7x zones in 2023. But unless shocks emerge, I feel the downside impact should be contained, and the income should help cushion any further multiple compression in anticipation of book value erosion. The key distinction versus 2023 is that the current setup does not combine rate pressure with visible funding and spread instability. Without that combination, the probability of a similar valuation extreme is lower, even if rates remain volatile.

Let us first understand the floor cushion in the stress scenario of the PB ratio reaching ~0.6x, as seen in 2023. This was a regime of historically sharp rate hikes alongside funding and spread instability. A reversal to even those valuations means ~35% downside from here, which can be cushioned by 2 years of current yields at ~16.5% (or a little more if the yields drop). So accumulation through rate risk stress is a hedged idea in my view.

Funding, Spreads and Earnings Transmissions

Given the income cushion and valuation discount, the rest of the thesis focuses on proving why the funding, spreads, and earnings environment is not yet broken enough to lead to a drastic valuation drop from here (something that takes 2 years of income cushion for recovery, as argued as the downside floor earlier).

One of the key arguments is that funding stress is not imminent due to the rate spikes recently. The funding stress of 2022-23 was primarily across the board, as rate spikes impacted short-term rates meaningfully. In this yield spike over the past month, the 1-year treasury yield has not reverted to the same levels as seen a year ago, like the 10-year yield has. Funding is repo-based for Dynex and hence more sensitive to short rate rates than 10-year yield moves.

According to management commentary in Q4 2025, the rate impact on returns is more nuanced than simply a rate view alone, that too as a single variable.

And as we look at each component of the yield curve, we're thinking a lot about, okay, the mortgage rate in isolation, the Fed funds policy rate, SOFR rates in isolation, those sorts of things.

We could see different parts of the rate complex behaving differently, like we are seeing now in terms of short-term versus long-term yield spikes, and that is not sufficient to call for funding cost worries. Short-term yields (for repricing borrower costs) and liquidity (for repo costs) should be monitored and could evolve differently than long-term yields (which have reverted to levels seen a year ago). Agency RMBS repo spreads remained in the ~15–20 bps range through Q4 2025 and into early 2026, indicating stable funding conditions. For material changes to that, we should see a reversal of the short-term rates to much higher levels than are being indicated today.

Spread impacts also look more contained today, even if stress worsens slightly. There has been a material shift in the structure of the mortgage markets, and balance sheet demand has returned.

Recent actions and guidance now point toward a more stable and supportive framework for the mortgage market. creating a strong foundation for forward returns and greater confidence in the path ahead for MBS spreads.

The re-expansion of GSE balance sheets introduces a more consistent source of demand. Overall, the current environment does not ensure that spreads cannot widen in an unstable way that forces deleveraging, but the conditions required for material and unstable widening (like seen in 2022-23) are more demanding than a simple rise in rates.

The earnings engine looks stable as a result of a relatively stable funding cost and spread environment. In the Q4 call, management guided to a mid-teens RoE (broadly a continuation of current strong RoE levels) at ~7x leverage. Importantly, that guidance did not assume any further favorable spread conditions. That framing reduces the ask to maintaining the current conditions (not further improvements) with some scope for minor stress leading to slightly lower but still healthy RoE.

A peer comparison reveals Dynex's relative discount to book valuation today, while peers with broadly similar agency mREIT models and similar yields are trading at modest premiums to book valuations. Dynex's leverage profile is also not looking riskier. At the operating level, we see a visibly improving earnings trajectory (as discussed in the next section), with normalized earnings now covering the dividend (normalized EPS ~$0.74 and dividend ~$0.51). That potentially indicates a valuation gap that is more based on scale and positioning rather than any structural drag and supports the Dynex Buy thesis.

Underlying Resilience

Growth in Dynex’s model is not about topline revenue but about how much balance sheet it can deploy into profitable spreads. Over the past year, total assets have expanded from roughly $8b to ~$17b, while earning assets have scaled to ~$16b. A large part of this expansion has come in a more favorable spread environment compared to 2023, when spreads were volatile and funding costs were rising sharply. This means the current asset base is not just larger but also deployed at better entry points. Higher starting yields can provide a buffer even if book value sees mark-to-market pressures. The recovery of net interest income and normalized earnings suggests that the balance sheet today should be more resilient to yield volatility than it was during prior tightening phases. The below chart shows not just balance sheet growth, but the fact that this expansion is now translating into positive net interest income (total assets as a reasonable proxy for deployment scale).

The financial metrics overall also show strong progression in recent quarters. Total revenues have scaled ~$200m from ~$60m a year ago. Net interest income has also turned meaningfully positive at ~$95m compared to negative or marginal levels through much of 2024. That supports a regime where the stock could be resilient to mark-to-market-driven sensitivities, having now transitioned to a more stable carry-driven income profile. Profitability has also followed the topline strength, with net income rising to ~$185m from ~$51m a year ago (normalized net income improved from ~$32m to ~$116m). This improvement is consistently seen sequentially too over the past few quarters. At the same time, operating expenses have increased at a much slower pace (to ~$16.6m in Q4 2025 from ~$9.2m a year ago), indicating operating leverage as the balance sheet scales.

Another point of resilience is the improving cash reserves over the past few quarters (~$1b including unencumbered assets). This gives it the flexibility to absorb market moves without having to sell assets at weak prices.

Accumulate and Monitor

Sensitivity to interest rates remains a relevant risk. A 100 bps increase in rates is estimated to impact book value by ~6-7%, with spread-widening scenarios contributing an additional ~8% downside. However, these sensitivities are largely mark-to-market and do not directly translate into earnings impairment unless we start seeing funding stress or forced deleveraging. In the current setup, where funding markets remain functional and spreads relatively stable, rate moves alone are more likely to be a source of valuation volatility rather than a material earnings risk.

The thesis breakers for my Buy Dynex call are not a short-term spike in long-term yields but rather more sinister developments such as material funding shocks that dislocate the repo markets, policy reversals that remove the current support for mortgage markets, or spreads returning to uncontrolled and unstable widening. Those risks or extreme market stress causing liquidity panic aside, Dynex should be accumulated whenever valuations dip further starting now.

The Buy thesis is well positioned because the earnings do not model further rate cuts already or a spread expansion. The policy support right now is also very strong and cushions tail risk possibilities. Long-term yields can impact valuation to an extent but should not impact the carry trade. The stock has fallen due to rate worries, but the actual earnings have not yet confirmed any earnings drop (and should have subdued impacts in my view). That gap between price and performance is what creates the opportunity here—not a turnaround, just mispricing.

Risk Scenarios and Re-evaluation Triggers

What matters more than the recent move in long-term yields is whether that volatility begins to transmit into funding costs and spreads. As discussed earlier, the current move in the yield curves reflects a reversal in trajectory rather than a fresh tightening shock, and in isolation should largely remain a book value headwind rather than an earnings risk impact. The sensitivity outlined above (~6-7% book value impact for a 100 bps move, with an additional ~8% under spread stress) also indicates that rates alone do not recreate the magnitude of downside seen in 2023, where valuation compressed toward ~0.6x PB alongside funding and spread instability. The income cushion argument therefore holds as long as this remains a mark-to-market cycle without forced balance sheet adjustments.

The conditions under which this changes are more specific and need to be monitored. A sustained repricing of short-end rates toward ~4.75-5% (1 year/SOFR), along with repo spreads moving meaningfully above the ~15-20 bps range into ~30-40 bps territory, could signal that funding costs are no longer stable.

Additionally, any shift where the yield curve adjustment is driven by the short end (rather than long-end volatility) or a material widening in agency MBS spreads (~50-75 bps) with signs of liquidity stress would indicate that rate moves are beginning to impair the carry trade rather than just book value. At current levels, short-end yields are still not at levels seen during the 2022-23 tightening phase, which suggests that the current move is not yet indicative of a funding stress regime that could correspond to stress valuations of ~0.6x PB (which was shown to be cushioned by ~2 years of income if the carry trade is intact).

This combination of factors—funding pressure, spread instability, and emerging earnings compression—would warrant a re-evaluation of the Buy thesis. Till such stress emerges, I see the current environment as one of valuation volatility rather than structural deterioration.

data.title: Ginkgo Bioworks: Shedding Biosecurity And Doubling Down On Autonomy
data.description:
Ginkgo Bioworks (DNA) reported soft results again in the fourth quarter of 2025, and made the decision to divest its biosecurity business. While cost-cutting efforts have reduced cash burn, breakeven is unlikely without significant growth, and Ginkgo's balance sheet continues to weaken.

I previously suggested that while Ginkgo was facing a weak demand environment, its biggest problem was a failure to find product-market fit. It is therefore not surprising that Ginkgo is enacting another strategic pivot to try and reinvigorate its business. The latest move is an attempt to capitalize on the emerging AI-enabled drug discovery opportunity and appears more likely to succeed. While Ginkgo is well positioned to capitalize on AI related demand, I remain neutral on the company's prospects until either the macro environment improves or Ginkgo's latest initiative demonstrates meaningful traction.

Market Conditions

Ginkgo continues to face a challenging demand environment, even if biopharma stocks rebounded somewhat in 2025. Funding for academic customers has been under pressure, and elevated interest rates generally remain an issue. While the impact of this has been fairly broad-based, Ginkgo's industrial and consumer businesses have been particularly hard hit.

There appears to be an emerging opportunity for companies that are able to support data generation for AI-enabled drug discovery though. For example, close to 40% of Twist Bioscience's (TWST) order growth in FY2025 was related to AI drug discovery, with customers including big tech companies like Google (GOOG).

Isomorphic Labs is a good illustration of the AI-enabled drug discovery opportunity. The company is focused on structure-based design using AI protein structure and binding-affinity prediction models. AlphaFold has been around for some time, but Isomorphic recently took another step forward with its Drug Design Engine, which improves generalizability and binding-affinity prediction. While Isomorphic Labs is focused on in-silico drug discovery, it still needs data to train and validate its models.

Ginkgo Bioworks Business Updates

Gingko's Cell Engineering business has shifted its focus towards projects that are smaller in scope in recent years, driven by its automation, AI, and Datapoints products. The results have been disappointing so far though, and Ginkgo is doubling down on autonomous labs in response. The company wants to demonstrate the capabilities of autonomous labs by moving its own R&D services into a single autonomous facility. Ginkgo will decommission its lab benches and replace them with work cells (100 RACs in the first half of 2026).

Customers can either utilize Ginkgo's in-house autonomous labs or purchase RACs directly. Ginkgo wants to drive adoption amongst large pharma companies, and hardware sales appear to be a focus of these efforts. Ginkgo already has a 47 million USD deal with Pacific Northwest National Labs (DoE) which will involve the installation of 97 RACs in an autonomous lab.

The company also wants to enable open-ended autonomous research through the use of AI. The company recently demonstrated this in collaboration with OpenAI. GPT-5 was used to design experiments, before submitting them to Ginkgo's autonomous lab in Boston. The lab would then send the results to GPT-5 and the model would iterate. This process resulted in the cost of cell-free protein synthesis being lowered 40%.

Ginkgo's Cell Engineering business had 109 revenue-generating programs at the end of 2025, a decline of 4% YoY. While Ginkgo has suggested that the drop is due to program rationalization as part of its restructuring efforts, it is hard not to feel that demand for the company's services remains weak. The Datapoints business managed to land 10 top pharma customers in its first full year though.

Gingko is also spinning off its biosecurity business, which will be taken private and operated independently. In return, Ginkgo will receive a 20% stake in the new company, Tower Biosecurity. While biosecurity has only ever been a small part of the Gingko story, it has been the primary driver of the company's financial success. The terms of the deal seem to indicate that Ginkgo no longer views this business as a priority and is primarily focused on eliminating biosecurity costs.

Financial Analysis

Ginkgo's Cell Engineering revenue totalled 26 million USD in the fourth quarter of 2025, down 26% YoY. Biosecurity revenue was only 7 million USD, although this is no longer much of a consideration.

Ginkgo hasn't provided revenue guidance for 2026, adding to investor uncertainty. Given the Biosecurity divestment and the current run-rate of the Cell Engineering business, it would not be surprising if revenue is down something like 25% YoY in 2026 though.

Ginkgo's Cell Engineering segment loss was 17 million USD in the fourth quarter. Cash burn dropped from 383 million USD in 2024 to 171 million USD in 2025, and totaled 47 million USD in the fourth quarter.

Cash burn is expected to further decline to 125-150 million USD in 2026. The shift towards autonomous labs will shrink Ginkgo's footprint, helping the company to reduce costs. The benefit of this will be somewhat muted while the commercial real estate market prevents Ginkgo from unloading much of this cost though. The carrying cost of excess lease space was 54 million USD in 2025 and Ginkgo has been fighting to reduce this for some time.

Cash burn isn't overly concerning given that Ginkgo retains over 423 million USD in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities. If the company can't start to generate meaningful growth over the next 1-2 years, its balance sheet is going to become a cause for investor concern though.

Conclusion

Ginkgo Bioworks has roughly a 400 million USD enterprise value versus something like a 100 million USD Cell Engineering revenue run rate. The company expects around 150 million USD cash burn in 2026 though, meaning its forward EV/S ratio is probably still around 5.5. This is still high for an unprofitable company that is yet to demonstrate consistent growth from its core business.

Ginkgo has now enacted 2 major strategic pivots to try and correct this. While move away from owning IP and introducing new products and services has had little impact so far, automation appears to be a more compelling value proposition. There is growing demand for data in support of AI at the moment, and Ginkgo is well positioned to capitalize on this. I remain neutral to negative on Ginkgo's prospects though, as growth is likely to disappoint in the near-term and losses are likely to remain elevated.

data.title: Moody's Corporation: Cheap Enough And Well-Protected
data.description:
It has been a little more than two years since I published my last article about Moody’s Corporation (MCO). While I always acknowledged that Moody’s Corporation was (and still is) a high-quality business, I also considered the stock price to always be a little too high over the last few years. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

I have been wrong in my last article and Moody's was once again able to reach its previous all-time high (set in late 2021). And now we saw the stock price bounce off that high once again - and in theory we could see a double-top with the risk of steeply declining stock prices.

There is one major risk I see - a long-lasting process of deleveraging especially in the United States - but even without the scenario of a major depression, the risk of a recession in combination with Moody's trading for high valuation multiples is posing a risk for the stock and makes lower stock prices in the coming quarters likely. Moody's is still a "Hold" for me. It is not a sell as I would seldom advice to bet against great companies with a wide economic moat - and Moody's is fitting that description perfectly.

Looking back at that statement, the “Hold” rating might have been correct to some degree, as Moody’s Corporation clearly underperformed the S&P 500 (18% total return for Moody’s vs. 32% return for the S&P 500). But I might have been a little too pessimistic in my last article.

In the last few weeks, Moody’s Corporation entered bear market territory again, losing more than 20% from its previous all-time high – in line with declines seen in several other software and financial services companies. In the following article I will explain why I think Moody’s Corporation might be at least fairly valued right now and why fears about GenAI might seem overblown.

Fiscal 2025 Results

We start by looking at the last reported results, and Moody’s Corporation performed well once again and beat analysts’ estimates for revenue as well as earnings per share – similar to previous quarters. Revenue in the fourth quarter increased from $1,672 million in Q4/24 to $1,889 million in Q4/25 – resulting in 13.0% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 37.3% year-over-year from $561 million in the same quarter last year to $770 million in this quarter. Finally, diluted earnings per share increased even by 57.1% year-over-year from $2.17 in Q4/24 to $3.41 in Q4/25.

For the full year, results are similar, and revenue increased 8.9% year-over-year from $7,088 million in fiscal 2024 to $7,718 million in fiscal 2025. Operating income also increased from $2,875 million in the previous year to $3,351 million in fiscal 2025, resulting in 16.6% year-over-year growth. Diluted earnings per share also increased 21.4% year-over-year from $11.26 in fiscal 2024 to $13.67 in fiscal 2025. Free cash flow also increased slightly from $2,521 million in the previous year to $2,575 million in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 2.1% year-over-year growth.

Moody’s At Risk?

In the last few weeks, I wrote several articles about software and financial services companies that seem to be under pressure. When looking for reasons, it is mostly stated that GenAI is threatening these businesses or that investors are seeing the threat of GenAI and therefore the stocks declined in the last few months.

And while Moody’s Corporation clearly underperformed the S&P 500 in the last few months, it performed better than many other software or financial services stocks. When looking at the performance over the last few months, we see Moody’s Corporation performing the best among the stocks in the chart below, as Moody’s Corporation declined “only” 20% from its previous all-time high. And while looking at the performance over the last three years, Moody’s Corporation increased 46% and clearly outperformed the others.

When looking at the stock performance of Moody’s Corporation vs. its peers, we can conclude that investors seem to be more confident about Moody’s Corporation and assume the company will perform better than some of its peers. In the following sections I will take a closer look at the company to answer the question of whether Moody’s Corporation is less at risk and might be a good investment.

When looking at revenue growth in the last few years, we see that growth rates are fluctuating heavily over the past decades. However, I can’t see hints for growth rates slowing down due to threats from GenAI. The slowdown in 2024 and 2025 can be explained by the typical fluctuations.

Aside from revenue growth, we can also look at the margins Moody reported in the last few years. And we clearly see gross margin and operating margin contracting in the quarters around 2023, but especially operating margin improved again in the recent past and is above the long-term average again. In my opinion, margins are also not giving us any clue that Moody’s Corporation might be under pressure due to increased competition from GenAI.

Finally, we are looking at the return on invested capital the company is reporting. And while we see RoIC declining over the last 15 years, we also see the numbers improving again in the last few quarters, and it is difficult to see any signs of Moody’s Corporation being under pressure at this point.

Qualitative Analysis

Analyzing different metrics is certainly providing us with many insights, and so far, we can conclude that Moody’s Corporation is performing stably and there are not really signs of the business being under pressure. But numbers are certainly not telling us the full story, and it might also take some time before metrics like revenue growth will show serious troubles for the business.

My approach here will be similar to the analysis of Morningstar (MORN) and S&P Global (SPGI) in the recent past. I will split up the business into its different segments and look at the business models individually to determine whether the risk of GenAI might be justified or not.

We start by looking at Moody’s Investors Service, the segment providing credit ratings. In fiscal 2025, this segment generated $4,119 million in revenue (about 53% of total revenue) and grew 8.6% year-over-year. And although this segment has a rather low part of recurring revenue (34% in fiscal 2025, 37% in Q4/25), I see this segment protected extremely well from competition. Together with S&P Global, Moody's is dominating this market – a market that is built on trust and based on the brand name of these companies. Competitors might be able to offer good (or maybe even great) credit ratings by using great scoring systems and GenAI, but these companies will have to prove over a long time that their ratings are reliable. And it is not enough for a few companies or individuals to accept ratings from a competitor – the entire financial world, which is now focused on ratings from Moody’s or S&P Global, has to agree that the ratings of some new competitor can also be trusted and widely accepted.

When looking at the second segment, Moody’s Analytics, revenue in fiscal 2025 increased 9.2% year-over-year to $3,599 million. Revenue from this segment is generated mostly by subscriptions, and therefore recurring revenue was 97% (which is usually a good sign for investors). Nevertheless, this segment seems to be more at risk from GenAI applications. The first threat is one I also mentioned in the article about several other software companies or providers of financial data – the shift away from a subscription-based revenue model, which would lead to a lower rate of recurring revenue and lower levels of predictability.

Several articles (including one from the Financial Times and the IDC) are indicating that seat-based subscription models might be replaced by new models. IDC is writing:

Such a shift has profound implications for how SaaS is bought and sold. The traditional per-user, per-month licensing model becomes increasingly obsolete as digital labor replaces manual interaction. IDC predicts that by 2028, pure seat-based pricing will be obsolete, with 70% of software vendors refactoring their pricing strategies around new value metrics, such as consumption, outcomes, or organizational capability.

The second threat is the ability of GenAI to replace some of the services and offerings in a similar and cheaper way. And while some offerings like data visualization or basic research might be replaceable by using publicly available data, we must be very clear about the limitations of GenAI. Not only are competitors missing the models to provide high-quality forecasting or credit ratings. An even bigger problem is the proprietary data that Moody’s Corporation is possessing and competitors are missing, and without the data, the best GenAI applications are useless.

Finally, we should also not ignore that Moody’s Corporation can implement GenAI applications into its services and offerings or use GenAI to improve its ratings process. In my opinion, GenAI will rather widen the economic moat around Moody’s Corporation and does not really seem like a threat to the business.

Expecting Double-Digit Growth

At this point, we have reasons to be optimistic about Moody’s Corporation for the years to come. And this optimism is also visible when looking at management’s guidance for fiscal 2026. Revenue is expected to grow in the high-single digits once again, and diluted earnings per share are expected to be in a range of $15.00 to $15.60. Compared to fiscal 2025, this would result in 10% to 14% bottom line growth. Adjusted diluted earnings per share are expected to be in a range of $16.40 to $17.00 – resulting also in 10% to 14% bottom line growth. Finally, free cash flow is expected to be in a range of $2.8 billion to $3.0 billion.

For the long run, management is also optimistic about revenue growth in the long run. The company’s long-term revenue growth algorithm for its MIS segment is leading to 6% to 9% growth for the years to come. Growth will stem from economic expansion, the company’s value proposition, and developing capital markets.

As far as I can tell, Moody’s Corporation did not issue a similar long-term growth target for its MA segment, but I would also argue that Moody’s Analytics is able to grow in the mid-to-high single digits as well (and growth rates might be even more stable). Aside from top line growth, Moody’s Corporation might also increase its margins a little bit (but I would not be too optimistic here).

Additionally, the company will continue to buy back shares, and this will have an impact on bottom-line growth. During the last 12 months, the number of diluted shares outstanding declined 1.7% to 178.7 million. Since the late 1990s, the number of shares outstanding decreased about 2.4% annually.

According to the company’s guidance, Moody’s Corporation will spend about $2 billion on share buybacks in fiscal 2026, and when using the current stock price, it would be enough to repurchase about 4.5 million shares and lower the share count by 2.5%

Combining this, we can assume that Moody’s Corporation will be able to grow in the low double digits, and these growth rates are in line with analysts’ assumptions for the years to come.

Between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2030, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 11.1% during these five years.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a final step, we must determine whether Moody’s Corporation is a good investment at this point or if the stock is still a bit too expensive to be a good investment. We can start by looking at the valuation multiples the stock is currently trading for. At the time of writing, Moody’s Corporation is trading for 31.3 times earnings as well as 29.9 times free cash flow.
